name: Vocabulary: Singular/Plural (including Zero Plural), Derivatives, and Abbreviations

sourceRefs.label: 2022_q page 23 Q84 (Furniture's zero-plural status, stashed earlier, with answer key)
civilserviceaspirants.in - dedicated TNPSC Singular/Plural/Zero-Plural MCQ set (6 real-style questions)
Common Latin-derived English abbreviations (e.g., i.e., etc., RSVP, ASAP, FYI)

researchNotes: Fifth Vocabulary topic, finally consuming the Furniture zero-plural fact stashed since Day-61-era research, plus a dedicated TNPSC-specific source with 6 directly relevant questions on zero-plural and irregular-plural nouns. Expansion pass (Batch 13): added more zero-plural nouns (deer, series, species), classic irregular plurals (child/mouse/tooth/foot), and further common Latin-derived abbreviations (N.B., P.S., a.m./p.m.).

reading.intro: Covers 'zero plural' nouns (same form in singular and plural, or always one form regardless of number), irregular plurals, and common abbreviations.

qa.question: Real exam: Is 'Furnitures' a correct plural form? What kind of noun is 'Furniture'?
qa.answer: NO — 'Furnitures' is incorrect. Furniture is an UNCOUNTABLE noun with NO plural '-s' form (a 'zero plural' noun) — confirmed by a real exam question, alongside Scissors, Pliers, and News, which are always-plural-FORM nouns that also don't change.

qa.question: Real exam: Is 'Data' treated as singular or plural in modern usage — "This data is important" or "This data are important"?
qa.answer: SINGULAR in standard modern usage: "This data IS important" — even though 'data' is historically the Latin plural of 'datum', it's now conventionally treated as a singular mass noun (similar to 'furniture').

qa.question: Real exam: Is 'Aircraft' the same in singular and plural? Give the correct usage.
qa.answer: Yes — 'Aircraft' is a ZERO PLURAL noun: "The AIRCRAFT ARE ready" (NOT 'aircrafts'), confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: How is 'Fish' pluralised when referring to multiple individual fish?
qa.answer: 'Fish' typically stays the SAME in the plural: "Five FISH were in the tank" (not 'fishes', though 'fishes' is occasionally used when referring to multiple different SPECIES of fish) — confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: How is 'Sheep' pluralised, and how is 'Goose' pluralised?
qa.answer: 'Sheep' stays the SAME in the plural: "one sheep, two sheep" (zero plural). 'Goose' has an IRREGULAR plural: "Geese" (not 'gooses') — both confirmed by real exam questions.

qa.question: What do the common Latin-derived abbreviations 'e.g.', 'i.e.', and 'etc.' stand for and mean?
qa.answer: e.g. (exempli gratia) = 'for example'. i.e. (id est) = 'that is' (used to clarify/restate, not give examples). etc. (et cetera) = 'and so on/and other similar things'.

qa.question: What do the abbreviations 'RSVP', 'ASAP', and 'FYI' stand for?
qa.answer: RSVP (Répondez s'il vous plaît, French) = 'please reply'. ASAP = 'as soon as possible'. FYI = 'for your information'.

qa.question: What is the correct plural of 'deer', 'series', and 'species' — a group of zero-plural nouns beyond the common animal examples?
qa.answer: DEER = same in singular and plural ('one deer, two deer'). SERIES = same in singular and plural ('one series, two series'). SPECIES = same in singular and plural ('one species, several species') — all three keep their exact form regardless of number.

qa.question: What are the irregular plurals of 'child', 'mouse', 'tooth', and 'foot'?
qa.answer: CHILD → CHILDREN. MOUSE → MICE. TOOTH → TEETH. FOOT → FEET — none simply add '-s', unlike the regular pattern.

qa.question: What do the abbreviations 'NB', 'P.S.', and 'a.m./p.m.' stand for?
qa.answer: N.B. (Nota Bene, Latin) = 'note well/take special notice'. P.S. (Post Scriptum, Latin) = 'written after' (used to add a note after signing a letter). a.m. (Ante Meridiem) = 'before noon'; p.m. (Post Meridiem) = 'after noon'.
